name: illegitimate children
shortDescription: In Roman mythology, there isn't a singular deity specifically dedicated to illegitimate children. However, certain goddesses, particularly Juno, held sway over aspects related to family and birth, indirectly influencing the lives of those born outside of wedlock.
description: While the Roman pantheon lacks a specific god or goddess directly overseeing illegitimate children, the circumstances surrounding their birth and lives were influenced by deities associated with family, marriage, and childbirth. Juno, as the queen of the gods and protector of marriage, ironically held a complex position. Though primarily concerned with legitimate unions and the sanctity of Roman families, her power over childbirth and the welfare of women meant that she indirectly impacted the lives of all children, regardless of their parents' marital status. The social stigma attached to illegitimate children in Roman society meant that their well-being was often precarious, and while no specific rituals or prayers were directed towards assisting them, individuals may have appealed to deities like Juno Lucina (Bringer of Light) for safe delivery, or to Fortuna for a chance at a better life, regardless of their origins. The absence of a dedicated deity highlights the Roman emphasis on legal marriage and legitimate offspring as cornerstones of their society. However, the inherent vulnerability of these children might have led to private devotions or appeals to various protective deities for aid and guidance, even if not formally recognized within the established religious framework.

sources.title: A History of Private Life, Vol I: From Pagan Rome to Byzantium
sources.author: Philippe Ariès and Georges Duby
